coredata.dc:title: Efficient red-emitting carbon dots for bioimaging and spectral engineering by polyetherimide modification
coredata.dc:creator: Fan, Jiaqi
Zhang, Jiahui
Huang, Wenjing
Zhou, Weiying
Zhou, Liya
Pang, Qi
Chen, Peican
Wu, Zhanchao
Mo, Fuwang
Xiao, Qiang
Zhang, Xinguo
coredata.dc:description: A novel type of red-emitting carbon dots was synthesized using urea and neutral red via the solvothermal method at a temperature of 200 °C for 4 h. The resulting CDs, named NR-CDs, display a distinct red emission at 620 nm when excited at a wavelength of 470 nm, achieving a quantum yield of 61.11% in acetone. Interestingly, NR-CDs exhibit strict solvent polarity dependence, i.e. the emission wavelength of NR-CDs red-shifts with an increase in solvent polarity. Besides, when incubated with NR-CDs and excited by an excitation wavelength of 470 nm, 4-T1 cells emit a clear red fluorescence. Meanwhile, high-quality in-vivo fluorescent images of zebrafish embryos and larvae were obtained by incubating with NR-CDs. Moreover, by modifying with polyetherimide (PEI), the peak value of PEI-NR-CDs shifts from 620 nm to 596 nm, and the emission is increased by 264 %. Co-localization experiment with commercial dye (Lyso-Tracker Green) shows that PEI-NR-CDs are localized in lysosomes and could be used as a lysosomal tracer for cells. Furthermore, PEI-NR-CDs were prepared into fluorescent ink, which exhibited orange fluorescence under UV light. All results demonstrate that as-obtained NR-CDs and PEI-NR-CDs can be used as reagent for fluorescent-imaging application.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Red emission
Bio-imaging
Spectral engineering

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Marking lysosomes in 4-T1 cells using PEI-NR-CDs
Optical imaging of NR-CDs in zebrafish embryos and larvae
Lysosomes marking and fluorescent ink application

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The research is funded by the Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA080005 ), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2022ME113 ), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260 ), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2020A1515110973 ) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) TEM image and HRTEM (insert) of NR-CDs. (B) FT-IR of NR-CDs. (C) Full XPS spectrum of NR-CDs. (D) XPS Cl 2p spectrum. (E) XPS C 1s spectrum. (F) XPS N 1s spectrum. (G) XPS O 1s spectrum.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectrum of NR-CDs in water. (B) Excitation and emission spectrum of NR-CDs in water. (Inset: the optical images of NR-CDs solution under daylight and 470 nm excitation wavelength). (C) The decay curve of NR-CDs in water. (D) The emission spectra of NR-CDs in different solvents under 470 nm excitation wavelength.
(A) Cytotoxicity effect of NR-CDs on 4-T1 cells. (B) Microscope images of 4-T1 cells incubated without NR-CDs. (C) Microscope images of 4-T1 cells incubated with 100 μg/mL NR-CDs.
(a) Bright field and (b) fluorescence field images of zebrafish embryos (24 hpf), which incubated with NR-CDs under different concentrations for different time.
Bright field images and fluorescence images were used to observe zebrafish cultured for 7 days with different concentrations of NR-CDs.
The normalized spectra (A), intensities (B), zeta (C), and lifetime (D) of NR-CDs modified with different volume of PEI under 470 nm excitation wavelength in water.
TEM image (A) and HRTEM (B) of PEI-NR-CDs. (C) FTIR of PEI-NR-CDs. (D) Full XPS spectrum of PEI-NR-CDs. (E)XPS Cl 2p spectrum. (F)XPS C 1s spectrum. (G)XPS N 1s spectrum. (H)XPS O 1s spectrum.

(A) Absorption, emission and excitation spectra of NR-CDs and PEI-NR-CDs in water. (Inset: the optical images of PEI-NR-CDs solution under daylight and 470 nm excitation wavelength). (B) The decay curve of PEI-NR-CDs in water. (C) PL spectra of PEI-NR-CDs at different excitation wavelengths in water. (D) The emission spectra of PEI-NR-CDs in different solvents. (E) The corresponding fluorescence photographs excited by 470 nm wavelength.
(A) Fluorescent images of PEI-NR-CDs and lyso tracker was performed at excitation wavelengths of 470 nm and 504 nm, respectively. Fluorescence ink images under sunlight (B) and UV light (C).
Synthesis, properties and application of NR-CDs and PEI-NR-CDs.

head.ce:title: Efficient red-emitting carbon dots for bioimaging and spectral engineering by polyetherimide modification

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Pulmonary and Critical Care Medicine, Shunde Hospital, Southern Medical University (the First People's Hospital of Shunde, Foshan), Foshan, 528300, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel type of red-emitting carbon dots was synthesized using urea and neutral red via the solvothermal method at a temperature of 200 °C for 4 h. The resulting CDs, named NR-CDs, display a distinct red emission at 620 nm when excited at a wavelength of 470 nm, achieving a quantum yield of 61.11% in acetone. Interestingly, NR-CDs exhibit strict solvent polarity dependence, i.e. the emission wavelength of NR-CDs red-shifts with an increase in solvent polarity. Besides, when incubated with NR-CDs and excited by an excitation wavelength of 470 nm, 4-T1 cells emit a clear red fluorescence. Meanwhile, high-quality in-vivo fluorescent images of zebrafish embryos and larvae were obtained by incubating with NR-CDs. Moreover, by modifying with polyetherimide (PEI), the peak value of PEI-NR-CDs shifts from 620 nm to 596 nm, and the emission is increased by 264 %. Co-localization experiment with commercial dye (Lyso-Tracker Green) shows that PEI-NR-CDs are localized in lysosomes and could be used as a lysosomal tracer for cells. Furthermore, PEI-NR-CDs were prepared into fluorescent ink, which exhibited orange fluorescence under UV light. All results demonstrate that as-obtained NR-CDs and PEI-NR-CDs can be used as reagent for fluorescent-imaging application.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as one of the most useful luminescent nanomaterials, have attracted tremendous attention [1]. In comparison to the conventional semiconductor nanocrystals for fluorescence, CDs possess advantages of low toxicity, high photostability, biocompatibility, low cost, and easy preparation [2,3]. Based on these excellent properties, CDs are widely applied in bioimaging [4–6], catalysis [7], sensing [8,9], and photocatalysis [10]. However, despite the advantages of CDs, achieving highly luminescent CDs emitting in the red and NIR regions has proven to be a challenge, and only a few successful attempts have been made so far. As a result, significant research efforts have been dedicated to developing CDs with red or NIR emission capabilities [11,12].

Compared to blue and green CDs, the optical imaging quality of long-wavelength emission (near infrared/deep infrared) is significantly improved. CDs with long-wavelength emissions also offer advantages such as deep penetration, minimal photodamage, and low radiation-induced damage. These qualities make them particularly suitable for applications in biological imaging and sensing [13,14]. Moreover, CDs of varying colors possess immense potential for utilization in various fields, including photocatalysis and light-emitting devices [15]. Various techniques can be employed to achieve spectral engineering, such as column chromatographic techniques [16] and acid reagent engineering [17]. However, the lack of a widely accepted theory on how CDs structures promote fluorescence makes it challenging to fine-tune the synthesis conditions for achieving the desired CDs fluorescence emission [18]. Thus, exploring an efficient and straightforward approach to regulate the emission spectrum is of great desirability.

Herein, we successfully synthesized NR-CDs through utilizing urea and neutral red (NR) as precursors under a facile and gentle hydrothermal treatment. By using NR-CDs as the fluorescent imaging agent, it is found that NR-CDs successfully penetrate the 4-T1 cells and the imaging results are satisfactory. Furthermore, because of the negligible cytotoxic and low autofluorescence background of NR-CDs, a safe and clear fluorescent imaging of zebrafish embryos and larvae could be realized. These findings suggest that the NR-CDs hold significant potential as a valuable fluorescent probe for bioimaging applications. Moreover, we found that the fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs can be significantly increased with blue-shift after modifying with polyetherimide (PEI). The PEI-NR-CDs exhibit a 264 % increase in emission intensity compared to NR-CDs. Based on the finding, it can be inferred that PEI modification has the potential to modulate the spectrum of CDs. Besides, PEI-NR-CDs also possess lysosome-targeting capability and can be successfully localized within lysosomes. Due to their excellent fluorescence properties, PEI-NR-CDs can be also employed in practical applications of fluorescent ink (see Scheme 1 ).

Urea and NR were purchased from Tanjin Damao Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd and Shanghai Macklin Biochemical Co. Ltd, respectively. PEI was obtained from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Co. Ltd. Acetone, ethanol, ethanediol were brought from Guangzhou GHTECH Co. Ltd. Lyso-Tracker Green was brought from Beyotime.

An American FEI Talos F200x was used to acquire transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of the CDs. American Thermo Scientific iN10 was applied to obtain the Fourier transforms infrared (FTIR) spectra of CDs. An American Thermo Scientific Nexsa was applied to acquire the X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Edinburgh FLS980 spectrometer was used to measure fluorescence spectra and lifetimes. Ultraviolet visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were measured using a Metash UV-5200 spectrophotometer. The zeta was tested by Zetasizer Nano ZS.

Optimization of NR-CDs: As seen from Fig. S1, the fluorescence spectra of NR-CDs synthesized using different ratios of NR and urea were measured. To obtain NR-CDs with red fluorescence, the ratio of NR and urea is selected as 1:10, which corresponds to the reaction proportion yielding the longest emission wavelength. Besides, the fluorescence spectra of NR-CDs synthesized at different temperatures were also measured (Fig. S2). It is observed that the NR-CDs exhibit the highest fluorescence intensity at 200 °C, leading to the selection of 200 °C as the reaction temperature.

Synthesis of optimized NR-CDs: NR (0.0435 g) and urea (0.0903 g) were sonicated in 30 mL of deionized water for 5 min after mixing. Subsequently, the mixture was transferred to a Teflon-lined autoclave and underwent a 4-h reaction at 200 °C. After cooling, the resulting solution, characterized by a red-brown color, was subjected to filtration through a 0.22 μm membrane. After the drying process, the pure NR-CDs were successfully obtained.

Synthesis of PEI-NR-CDs: The optimized NR-CDs (0.0100 g) was mixed with varying volumes (0–350 μL) of PEI in 10 mL water, followed by a solvothermal reaction at 220 °C for 4 h. The reaction mixture was filtered through a membrane (0.22 μm) after completion of the reaction. When 250 μL of PEI was added, the fluorescence intensity of the CDs reached its maximum, and the resulting CDs were named PEI-NR-CDs.

All decay curves are fitted well to a double exponential decay model:

)

The luminescence intensity (I) is modeled using a bi-exponential decay model, where τ1 and τ2 represent the life components, A1 and A2 represent constants, and t represents time. The average decay lifetime values are calculated using the following formula:
τ
a v g
=
A 1
τ 1
2
+
A 2
τ 2
2
A 1
τ 1
+
A 2
τ 2

The fluorescence QY of NR-CDs was investigated by means of an integrating sphere coupled with an FLS980 fluorescence spectrophotometer. Initially, NR-CDs solutions with varying solvents were prepared at a concentration of 0.03 mg/mL. Subsequently, the corresponding emission spectra were acquired for each solution. Finally, the QY of each sample was determined using fluorescence software based on photoluminescence measurements.

4-T1 Cells were seeded at a density of 1 × 105/mL in 96-well plates and incubated in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C for 24 h. Next, after discarding the original medium, 100 mL of medium containing different concentrations (0.075–5 mg/mL) of NR-CDs were added into the designated wells and the 4-T1 cells were further incubated for 24 h. The control group was treated with medium only. Thereafter, each well was treated with 10 μL of MTT and incubated for 4 h. After discarding the solutions from all the well, the formazan complex was dissolved by adding 150 μL of dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO). Eventually, the absorbance at 470 nm was measured using a microplate reader (Biotek, USA).

Seeded in a glass bottom cell culture dish, the 4-T1 cells were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C in Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) with 10% fetal bovine serum (FBS). Subsequently, the 4-T1 cells were incubated for 1 h with NR-CDs at 100 μg/mL. Afterwards, the supernatant was discarded, and 4-T1 cells were rinsed with phosphate-buffered saline (PBS) and suspended in PBS. Finally, all images were captured by using a confocal microscope.

After spawning, the embryos were removed and transferred to a Petri dish containing embryo culture liquid. The normal embryos were chosen 24 h post-fertilization. Firstly, 50 embryos were taken from each dish and placed in six-well plates, and different concentrations (8–250 μg/mL) of NR-CDs were added into the well and incubated for different time (15–240 min). After repeated washing, NR-CDs in the solution were removed, and then observed under a fluorescence inverted microscope. The same methods were used to compare the imaging effects of larvae after 7 days of normal culture. Larvae should be anesthetized with MS-222 anesthetic (anesthetic: water = 1:2500) before being observed under fluorescent inverted microscope.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Marking lysosomes in 4-T1 cells using PEI-NR-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: The cells were cultured overnight in DMEM medium containing 10% FBS, 1% streptomycin, and 1% penicillin in a 5% CO2 incubator at 37 °C. After diluting the PEI-NR-CDs solution by a hundredfold, it was added to the cell-containing DMEM medium and incubated for 4 h. Following three washes with PBS, the cells were then incubated with Lyso Tracker Green (50 nM) in the dark for 20 min. After careful washing, fluorescence imaging was performed on a confocal microscope at excitation wavelengths of 470 nm and 504 nm, respectively.

The blue-emitting carbon dots (B-CDs) and green-emitting carbon dots (G-CDs) were prepared according to our previous report [19]. 2 g polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) was added to 30 mL of H2O and stirred at 85 °C until a colloidal solution of PVA was obtained. Subsequently, the solution was cooled to 30 °C. To prepare PEI-NR-CDs ink, 300 μL PEI-NR-CDs solution and 1700 μL water were added to 1 mL PVA solution, followed by agitation to ensure thorough mixing. Similarly, the B-CDs ink was prepared by combining 10 μL B-CDs solution with 1990 μL water and adding them to 1 mL PVA solution, followed by thorough mixing through agitation. Lastly, the G-CDs ink was prepared by adding 200 μL G-CDs solution (1 mg/mL) and 1800 μL water to 1 mL PVA solution, which was then subjected to agitation to ensure complete homogenization.

The morphology of the NR-CDs was observed using TEM. As seen from Fig. 1 A, NR-CDs are uniform and demonstrate exceptional mono dispersity with the average lateral size of 5.3 nm. The high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) image illustrates that NR-CDs have a particular crystal structure with the well-defined lattice spacing of 0.22 nm, which is assigned to the (100) facet of graphitic. These results indicate that NR-CDs possess uniform nanoparticles distribution and excellent dispersibility. The surface composition and elements of NR-CDs were characterized by FT-IR and XPS. In FT-IR spectrum (Fig. 1B), the surface of NR-CDs contains N–H (3318 cm−1) [20], C–H (2972 cm−1) [21], C–H (2885 cm−1) [14], C–N (1385 cm−1) [22], C–O (1049 cm−1) [23] and Ar(σC-H) (869 cm−1) [20]. The full XPS spectrum (Fig. 1C) shows four signals at 196.3, 284.1, 400.0 and 532.9 eV, confirming that NR-CDs contain Cl, C, N, and O elements. Two peaks at 197.58 and 199.18 eV (Fig. 1D) could be assigned to the C–Cl and Cl 2p 3/2 bonding [24,25]. In case of C 1s (Fig. 1E), two peaks at 284.28 and 285.18 eV are due to C–C and C–O, respectively [21,26]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 1F) shows three peaks at 398.98, 401.28 and 406.58 eV from pyridine N, graphitic N and N–H, respectively [20,21,27]. The O 1s spectrum (Fig. 1G) exhibits two peaks at 531.58 eV for CO and 532.58 eV for C–O [21,26].

The luminescent properties of NR-CDs was investigated using UV–Vis absorption spectrum, fluorescence spectra and lifetime decay measurements. Fig. 2 A shows that the NR-CDs exhibit two absorption peaks at 269 nm and 520 nm, corresponding to the π–π* transition of the CO bond and the n–π* transition of the CO and C–O/C–N bonds [22,28]. As exhibited in Fig. 2B, NR-CDs demonstrate a peak fluorescence wavelength at 620 nm with optimal excitation at 470 nm, achieving a quantum yield of 15.96% in water. Compared to the original raw material NR, NR-CDs exhibit a significant enhancement in fluorescence intensity (Fig. S3). As seen from Fig. S4, when the excitation wavelength is altered, the emission wavelength of NR-CDs shows minimal change, indicating NR-CDs is excitation-independent in water. Moreover, due to the excellent solubility of NR-CDs in DMF, DMF is chose as the solvent to measure the emission spectra of NR-CDs at different concentrations. As shown in Fig. S5, fluorescence quenching of NR-CDs occurs at high concentrations, which could be attributed to self-absorption and fluorescence resonance energy transfer effects in NR-CDs. Furthermore, the biexponential formula is used to fit the fluorescence decay curve of NR-CDs, revealing an average PL lifetime of 2.97 ns (Fig. 2C).

Interestingly, it has been found that the emission wavelength of NR-CDs can vary in different solvents (Fig. 2D). The transition of emission color from green to red in NR-CDs can be attributed to the increasing solvent polarity, which is likely a result of the pronounced interaction between surface groups on the NR-CDs and solvent molecules [29]. The interaction between NR-CDs and protic solvents (such as ethanol, ethanediol, and water) involves hydrogen bonding (HB), which effectively enhances the carrier density. This interaction leads to a progressive downward modulation of the energy levels associated with the excited states [30]. In the case of aprotic solvents (such as acetone), the electrons stabilize themselves by inducing changes in the dipole moment of the solvent molecules. With the enhancement of solvent polarity, there is an increased transfer of excited state charges from the functional group to the neighboring solvent molecules. As a consequence, in high-polarity solvents, the excited electrons undergo more significant energy dissipation compared to low-polarity solvents, resulting in a redshift in the emission spectrum [31]. As shown in Fig. S6, the acceptance of HB by NR-CDs is notably enhanced upon photoexcitation, potentially owing to an augmentation of the negative charge density present on the surface of the HB acceptor groups [29]. The observed reduction in charge density provides evidence supporting the heightened density of electron clouds in solvents of high polarity, thereby promoting long-wavelength emission [32]. Furthermore, as the solvent polarity increases, the fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs gradually decreases. The QY of NR-CDs in acetone is 61.11%, while in water it is only 15.96%. This could be attributed to the fact that as the solvent polarity increases, NR-CDs exhibit a stronger dipole moment in the ground state compared to the excited state and have a faster non-radiative decay rate, resulting in a decrease in fluorescence intensity [33].

Given their impressive optical performance, NR-CDs seem to be a suitable choice for live cell imaging. To confirm the possible application of NR-CDs in biological systems, the cytotoxicity of the NR-CDs was evaluated. As seen from Fig. 3 A, after culturing 4-T1 cells with 100 μg/mL NR-CDs for 24 h, it is evident that the NR-CDs exhibit minimal toxicity to the 4-T1 cells. This finding further demonstrates the low toxicity and excellent biocompatibility of NR-CDs. Therefore, the application of NR-CDs for imaging in 4-T1 cells was examined.

After incubation with 100 μg/mL NR-CDs solutions for 1 h, the 4-T1 cells were imaged under 470 nm laser excitation using a confocal microscope. As seen from Fig. 3B–C, the red fluorescence from the 4-T1 cells incubated with NR-CDs are clearly observed, while the 4-T1 cells incubated without NR-CDs show no fluorescence. Furthermore, it is obvious that fluorescence comes from the cytoplasm of 4-T1 cells, which indicates that NR-CDs possess great biocompatibility and can easily penetrate the cell membrane to enter the cell cytoplasmic.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical imaging of NR-CDs in zebrafish embryos and larvae
ce:section.ce:para: Encouraged by the results of cell imaging, the applicability of NR-CDs in vivo was continued to be explored. Due to its optical transparency and rapid growth, the zebrafish is an excellent and typical vertebrate mode [27,34,35]. Therefore, zebrafish has been widely selected for in vivo imaging. As seen from Fig. 4 , the control groups show no fluorescence under 470 nm excitation. However, after incubating with NR-CDs, zebrafish embryos show an excellent red fluorescence, indicating the successful penetration of NR-CDs through the chorionic membrane [36]. Furthermore, as the concentration of NR-CDs and incubation time increases, the fluorescence intensity shows a gradual enhancement, and attains a maximum at an incubation time of 4 h and a NR-CDs concentration of 0.25 mg/mL. Zebrafish remained alive even when the concentration of NR-CDs reached 0.25 mg/mL, and no developmental damage has been observed (survival rate >90%), indicating the low cytotoxicity and great biocompatibility of NR-CDs.

To further explore the biological imaging, NR-CDs were also applied to larvae. It is found that the fluorescence of NR-CDs and larvae reach the maximum when they are incubated for 6 h from the previous experiments. As shown in Fig. 5 , there is no fluorescence in the control group under 470 nm whereas the strong red fluorescence of the larvae incubated with NR-CDs can be seen clearly. The fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs increases with an increase in its concentration. When the concentration of NR-CDs up to 0.25 mg/mL, the fluorescence reaches maximum. The fluorescence characteristics of NR-CDs exhibit a polarity-dependent behavior, manifesting as weak fluorescence in highly polar environments and strong red fluorescence in low-polarity systems. This polarity dependence arises from the inherent properties of NR-CDs. Consequently, the yolk sacs of larvae, which are abundant in lipids, demonstrate a prominent and discernible red fluorescence signal attributed to the strong fluorescence exhibited by NR-CDs in such lipid-rich environments [27]. These results further indicate that NR-CDs can achieve polarity selective imaging of zebrafish. At optimal concentration, the NR-CDs show negligible toxicity and will not influence the activity of zebrafish. Therefore, NR-CDs are expected to be an efficient and biologically safe reagent for in vivo and vitro fluorescent-imaging application.

Fluorescence spectral engineering is one of the most promising approaches to broaden the applications of CDs [37]. Usually, it is significant to modify the surface of CDs for their applications. At present, several modified CDs agents have been reported, such as Polyethylene imine (PEI) [37], 4,7,10-trioxa-1,13tridecanediamine (TTDDA) [38] and polyethylene glycol (PEG) [39]. The results show that the luminescent characteristics of CDs are significantly influenced by the presence of various additives [40]. Among the different additives, PEI, as a large molecule with a positive charge with a polyamine architecture, interacts strongly with anionic proteins on the cell surface [41]. Therefore, using PEI as a modifier to realize spectral engineering in an economical, simple and green way is necessary.

Fig. 6 A shows that, as the volume of PEI increased from 0 to 350 μL, the emission wavelengths gradually shifted from 618 to 595 nm towards the blue end of the spectrum. As seen from Fig. 6C, it can be observed that the surface of NR-CDs is negatively charged. With an increasing amount of PEI added, the surface of NR-CDs exhibits a gradual reduction in negative charge. Therefore, it is inferred that when an increased amount of PEI is added, a decrease in electron cloud density occurs, resulting in a decrease in the acceptability of PEI-NR-CDs for HB, leading to a blue shift in the emission wavelength [32].

At the same time, of particular interest is the observation that the fluorescent intensity of NR-CDs undergoes a significant enhancement following PEI modification. Compared to NR-CDs, the emission intensity of PEI-NR-CDs is significantly enhanced, particularly with a 264% increase in a 250 μL PEI solution (Fig. 6B). It might be due to the strong interaction between NR-CDs with PEI. The fluorescence from the core and surface state of pure CDs is significantly quenched by strong interactions with water molecules in an aqueous environment. After modifying with PEI, undergoes functionalization through the attachment of PEI molecules, resulting in the effective hindrance of the interaction between NR-CDs and water molecules, and decrease the surface electron withdrawing groups [40].

Furthermore, Fig. 6D shows the lifetime of NR-CDs added with different volume of PEI. As the volume of PEI rises from 150 μL to 300 μL, the lifetime of NR-CDs increases. These results also suggest that the PEI strongly interacts with NR-CDs. However, when NR-CDs modified with 350 μL PEI, the lifetime reduces to 2.58 ns. The weak fluorescence intensity of NR-CDs in high concentration PEI solutions may be attributed to the significant freedom of additional PEI chains within the water droplets. As a result, the interaction between the amine groups of PEI and the CD surface is weakened, leading to a weaker fluorescence signal [37].

The morphology of newly synthesized PEI-NR-CDs have been characterized by TEM. As seen from Fig. 7 A, PEI-NR-CDs have great dispersibility and uniform distribution. The HRTEM (Fig. 7B) further indicates that the discernible lattice fringe (0.16 nm) of PEI-NR-CDs, which corresponds well to the (100) crystal facet lattice of graphene. Through the FT-IR and XPS, the surface composition and elements of PEI-NR-CDs are characterized. In the FT-IR spectrum of PEI-NR-CDs, the peaks around 3314 cm−1 and 2972 cm−1 belong to the –NH and C–H bonds, respectively (Fig. 7C) [20,21]. The peak at 2879 cm−1 is owing to C–H bond [14]. The C–N bonds are formed at 1382 cm−1 [42]. The absorption peak at 1045 cm−1 is ascribed to the vibrations of C–O [23]. The peak at 872 cm−1 is attributed to the presence of Ar(σC-H) [20]. The wide-scan XPS spectrum (Fig. 7D) shows four major binding energy peaks at 197.7, 286.7, 397.5, and 530.3 eV for Cl, C, N and O, respectively. As for the Cl 2p spectrum (Fig. 7E), the peaks at 196.38 eV and 197.98 eV are attributed to C–Cl and Cl 2p 3/2 bonding, respectively [24,25]. Moreover, there are two peaks at 284.38 eV and 284.98 eV in the C 1 s spectrum (Fig. 7F), which are attributed to C–C and C–O, respectively [20,26]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 7G) shows two peaks, which are assigned to C–N–C at 397.98 eV and pyridine at 398.58 eV [20,26]. As seen in Fig. 7H, two subpeaks from the O 1s peaks are at 530.28 eV (CO) and 531.28 eV (C–O) [22,26].

To further explore the optical properties of PEI-NR-CDs, investigations were conducted on the UV–Vis and fluorescent spectra. As seen from Fig. 8 A, there are two peaks around 277 nm and 461 nm, which are ascribed to n–π* transition and the π–π* transition. Next, the emission performance was characterized. With excitation at 470 nm, PEI-NR-CDs exhibit an emission peak at 596 nm. Moreover, Fig. 8B shows that decay curve of PEI-NR-CDs is well-fitted to a double exponential function. The average fluorescence lifetime of PEI-NR-CDs is found to be 3.56 ns. In addition, at different excitation wavelengths, PEI-NR-CDs show excitation-independent feature (Fig. 8C). The maximum fluorescent emission wavelength of 596 nm is found to be maintained despite the increase in excitation wavelength from 410 nm to 550 nm. The emission spectra of PEI-NR-CDs in different solvents were also investigated. As exhibited in Fig. 8D, when PEI-NR-CDs are dispersed in water, ethanediol, ethanol and acetone, the emission wavelength is 596 nm, 582 nm, 574 nm, 540 nm, respectively. The corresponding set of PL photos of PEI-NR-CDs solutions are shown in Fig. 8E.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Lysosomes marking and fluorescent ink application
ce:section.ce:para: Lysosomes are critical organelles containing various hydrolytic enzymes that are involved in the degradation of cellular substances [43]. They play a crucial role in maintaining cellular homeostasis through processes such as autophagy, cell apoptosis, and other biological processes [44]. Therefore, real-time monitoring of lysosomal dynamics in live cells is of significant importance for a deeper understanding of their physiological and pathological functions [45]. Based on the excellent cell imaging performance of NR-CDs, to explore the subcellular distribution of PEI-NR-CDs within cells, co-localization experiments were conducted using a commercially available lysosomal localization dye (Lyso-Tracker green) for further characterization. As shown in Fig. 9 , a strong overlap between the red fluorescence emitted by PEI-NR-CDs and the green fluorescence emitted by tracker is observed, confirming the mitochondria co-localization property of PEI-NR-CDs. The results provide evidence that PEI-NR-CDs possess effective distribution within cells for polarity tracking and imaging purposes.

Due to the excellent luminescent properties of PEI-NR-CDs in water, they were fabricated into fluorescent ink. As shown in Fig. 9B–C, fluorescent inks prepared from PEI-NR-CDs, G-CDs, and B-CDs were uniformly coated onto non-fluorescent filter paper. When exposed to sunlight, the fluorescent ink prepared from PEI-NR-CDs exhibits a pale-yellow color. However, under ultraviolet irradiation, the PEI-NR-CDs exhibit bright fluorescence. Based on the aforementioned analysis, the PEI-NR-CDs demonstrate promising potential for applications in the printing industry's fluorescent patterns, owing to their favorable optical properties and cost-effectiveness.

In summary, urea and neutral red were used to synthesize novel CDs with bright red emission via a hydrothermal method. At 470 nm excitation, the resulting CDs, named NR-CDs, show broadband red emission peak at 620 nm. In addition, upon incubation with NR-CDs, 4-T1 cells and zebrafish display a distinct red fluorescence when excited by a 470 nm wavelength. This indicates that NR-CDs not only exhibit excellent imaging depths but also possess significant biocompatibility. In conclusion, the NR-CDs can be a promising reagent for in vivo and in vitro fluorescent-imaging application. Furthermore, an efficient strategy to realize spectral engineering of CDs is demonstrated. Compared to NR-CDs, PEI can significantly enhance the intensity by up to 264% and cause a blue-shift of 24 nm by preventing the interaction between NR-CDs and water molecules. Besides, the PEI-NR-CDs can successfully localize within the lysosomes, demonstrating its potential as a lysosomal tracer for cellular applications. Furthermore, PEI-NR-CDs can be prepared as fluorescent ink for its excellent fluorescent properties in water. All results indicate that NR-CDs and PEI-NR-CDs can be utilized as fluorescent imaging reagents.

Jiaqi Fan: Investigation, Writing – original draft. Jiahui Zhang: Investigation. Wenjing Huang: Methodology. Weiying Zhou: Visualization. Liya Zhou: Visualization. Qi Pang: Resources. Peican Chen: Resources. Zhanchao Wu: Funding acquisition. Fuwang Mo: Funding acquisition. Qiang Xiao: Writing – review & editing. Xinguo Zhang: Conceptualization, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The research is funded by the Science Foundation of Guangxi Province (No. 2022GXNSFDA080005), Shandong Provincial Natural Science Foundation (No. ZR2022ME113), Guangzhou Scientific planning program (No. 201804010260), Guangdong Basic and Applied Basic Research Foundation (No. 2020A1515110973) and National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21601081).
